passages.explanation: Jesus enters Capernaum. A centurion's servant, whom he values highly, is sick and about to die. The centurion sends Jewish elders to ask Jesus to heal his servant. They plead earnestly: this man deserves your help — he loves our nation and built our synagogue. Jesus goes with them. But when he's not far from the house, the centurion sends friends with a message: Lord, don't trouble yourself — I don't deserve to have you come under my roof. That's why I didn't even consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and my servant will be healed. I'm a man under authority with soldiers under me. I tell one 'go' and he goes, another 'come' and he comes. Jesus is amazed and tells the crowd: I tell you, I haven't found such great faith even in Israel. The messengers return and find the servant well. The centurion understands authority — he recognises that Jesus commands sickness the way he commands soldiers. No physical presence needed.

Jesus goes to a town called Nain, with his disciples and a large crowd. As he approaches the town gate, a dead man is being carried out — the only son of a widow. A large crowd from the town is with her. When the Lord sees her, his heart goes out to her. He says: don't cry. He goes up and touches the coffin. The bearers stand still. He says: young man, I say to you, get up! The dead man sits up and begins to talk. Jesus gives him back to his mother. Everyone is filled with awe and praises God: a great prophet has appeared among us — God has come to help his people. This news spreads throughout Judea and the surrounding country. Jesus isn't asked to help — he sees a grieving mother and acts. The miracle echoes Elijah raising the widow's son in 1 Kings 17. The crowd's conclusion is right: God has visited his people.

John the Baptist's disciples tell him about all these things. John sends two of them to ask Jesus: are you the one who is to come, or should we expect someone else? When they arrive, Jesus is curing diseases, sicknesses, and evil spirits, and giving sight to the blind. He replies: go back and report to John what you've seen and heard — the blind receive sight, the lame walk, those with leprosy are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is proclaimed to the poor. Blessed is anyone who does not stumble on account of me. Jesus doesn't answer with a yes — he points to the evidence, which maps directly onto Isaiah 35 and 61. John, sitting in prison, is asking an honest question. Jesus honours it with proof, not rebuke.

After John's messengers leave, Jesus speaks to the crowd about John. What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed swaying in the wind? A man dressed in fine clothes? Those who wear expensive clothes and indulge in luxury are in palaces. A prophet? Yes, and more than a prophet. This is the one about whom it is written: I will send my messenger ahead of you, who will prepare your way before you. Among those born of women there is no one greater than John — yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he. All the people and the tax collectors acknowledged God's justice, having been baptised by John. But the Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God's purpose for themselves, not having been baptised by John. Jesus compares this generation to children sitting in the marketplace calling to each other: we played the pipe for you and you didn't dance; we sang a dirge and you didn't cry. John came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say he has a demon. The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and you say he's a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners. But wisdom is proved right by all her children. The critics reject both John's asceticism and Jesus' socialising — the problem isn't the style, it's the message.

A Pharisee named Simon invites Jesus to eat with him. Jesus reclines at the table. A woman in that town who has lived a sinful life learns that Jesus is eating at the Pharisee's house. She brings an alabaster jar of perfume, stands behind him at his feet weeping, wets his feet with her tears, wipes them with her hair, kisses them, and pours perfume on them. Simon thinks: if this man were a prophet, he'd know what kind of woman is touching him. Jesus responds with a parable: two people owed money to a moneylender. One owed five hundred denarii, the other fifty. Neither could pay, so he cancelled both debts. Which will love him more? Simon answers correctly: the one who had the bigger debt cancelled. Jesus turns to the woman and says to Simon: you didn't give me water for my feet, but she wet them with her tears. You didn't give me a kiss, but she hasn't stopped kissing my feet. You didn't put oil on my head, but she poured perfume on my feet. Her many sins have been forgiven — as her great love has shown. Whoever has been forgiven little loves little. Then he says to her: your sins are forgiven. The other guests murmur: who is this who even forgives sins? Jesus tells the woman: your faith has saved you. Go in peace. The woman understands what Simon doesn't — the size of the debt cancelled determines the depth of the gratitude.
